Cloudflare.raw_input:
13:42 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Anycast IP space unreachable from multiple external probes. Global synthetic checks failing.
13:45 UTC - Edge ingress traffic drops to near 0. Internal monitoring losing telemetry from all edge PoPs. This is not regional.
13:49 UTC - Trying to SSH to SJC/LHR/NRT edge routers. High packet loss, then total connection timeout. Out-of-band (OOB) console connections also hanging.
13:54 UTC - Declaring Sev-1/P0. Spinning up incident bridge. Internal comms struggling, switching to backup Zoom.
13:58 UTC - Transit partners (Level3, Telia) reporting our BGP sessions are down/flapping globally. Edge routers seem completely dead or isolated.
14:04 UTC - Finally got console access on a router in SJC. Control plane CPU is at 100%, BGP daemon is unresponsive. Console barely accepting keystrokes.
14:10 UTC - Team looking at recent automated pipeline runs. Attempting hard power cycle of SJC edge router to see if it recovers or stays down.
Cloudflare.description: A bad config (router rule) caused all of their edge routers to crash, taking down all of Cloudflare.

generated.summary: A global edge network outage occurred due to a control plane failure on edge routers, resulting in a total loss of ingress traffic and BGP session instability.
generated.root_cause: A malformed automated configuration deployment triggered a control plane CPU exhaustion spike across the global edge router fleet, rendering BGP daemons unresponsive and causing BGP sessions to flap or fail.
timeline: 13:42 UTC: Global synthetic checks and Anycast IP space unreachable; PagerDuty alerts triggered.
13:45 UTC: Edge ingress traffic dropped to near zero; internal telemetry from edge PoPs lost.
13:49 UTC: SSH and OOB console connections to edge routers became unresponsive.
13:54 UTC: Incident declared as Sev-1/P0; bridge established.
13:58 UTC: Transit partners confirmed global BGP session failure.
14:04 UTC: OOB console access confirmed SJC router control plane CPU at 100%.
14:10 UTC: Investigation shifted to automated configuration pipeline; SJC router hard power cycle initiated.
generated.impact: Global ingress traffic was completely halted, resulting in a total service outage for all end-users during the incident window.
action_items: Implement canary deployments for all network configuration changes to prevent global blast radius.
Introduce control plane CPU protection policies to prioritize OOB access during resource exhaustion.
Conduct a root cause analysis of the automated pipeline to identify the specific malformed configuration.
Establish an out-of-band management network independent of the primary control plane for emergency recovery.

Cloudflare.reason: The AI accurately identifies the core root cause (bad configuration deployment) and the resulting impact (total global edge router failure and outage) described in the ground truth.

Google.raw_input:
08:12 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Multiple core production services reporting connection timeouts. Uptime checks failing for all workloads on GCVE.
08:17 UTC - Verified network path. Interconnect is up, but cannot ping GCVE vCenter or NSX-T endpoints. Port 443 timed out.
08:25 UTC - Logged into GCP Console. GCVE portal is completely empty. Private Cloud "us-east1-pc" is not listed. No resources visible.
08:31 UTC - Checking Cloud Audit Logs for IAM deletions. Filter: protoPayload.serviceName="vmwareengine.googleapis.com" and protoPayload.methodName:"delete". Zero results. No user initiated a delete.
08:40 UTC - Opened P1 Support Case with Google (Case #823910). Escalated to TAM.
08:52 UTC - Internal sync: Confirmed with infra team that no decommissioning work or migrations were scheduled. VMs are completely dark, storage unreachable.
09:05 UTC - Google Support acknowledges. State on their end shows the private cloud status as "DELETED" but they are investigating why it triggered without user intervention.
Google.description: Initial GCVE provisioning was performed with a legacy option, which lead to a 'fixed term' contract with automatic deletion at the end of that period.

generated.summary: A critical production outage occurred when the GCVE Private Cloud environment was unexpectedly moved to a 'DELETED' state, resulting in total loss of connectivity and resource availability.
generated.root_cause: An internal Google Cloud platform error caused the automated deletion of the 'us-east1-pc' Private Cloud environment, despite no user-initiated IAM actions or decommissioning requests.
timeline: 08:12 UTC - Widespread connectivity timeouts and failed uptime checks detected for GCVE workloads.
08:17 UTC - Verified network path failure to vCenter and NSX-T endpoints.
08:25 UTC - Confirmed GCVE Private Cloud 'us-east1-pc' missing from GCP console.
08:31 UTC - Audit logs verified no user-initiated deletion commands were issued.
08:40 UTC - P1 support case opened with Google; escalated to TAM.
09:05 UTC - Google confirmed internal system state as 'DELETED' and commenced investigation.
generated.impact: All production services hosted on the GCVE 'us-east1-pc' cluster were offline, rendering internal core services and customer-facing workloads unreachable for the duration of the incident.
action_items: Follow up with Google Cloud TAM for the formal Root Cause Analysis (RCA) regarding the phantom deletion.
Evaluate cross-region disaster recovery options for critical GCVE workloads to mitigate single-provider regional failures.
Implement enhanced monitoring for GCVE environment status to reduce MTTR in the event of platform-level resource disappearance.

Google.reason: The AI incorrectly identifies the root cause as an internal platform error rather than the identified legacy configuration option that triggered a fixed-term contract expiration.

Amazon.raw_input:
03:12 UTC - SEV-1 Alert: High rate of DNS resolution timeouts detected in ap-northeast-2. Multiple downstream services reporting database and API connection errors.

03:18 UTC - Internal Slack channels flooding. Core application servers failing target-group health checks because they cannot resolve internal VPC endpoints. Confirmed local VPC resolver (169.254.169.253) is timing out on queries.

03:30 UTC - Paging Networking/Route53 on-call. Testing from sandbox instance in Seoul: dig commands to both public and private zones are failing. Resolver fleet metrics show active host count has plummeted.

03:48 UTC - Confirmed resolver fleet size in ap-northeast-2 is extremely low. Unclear why auto-scaling isn't replacing unhealthy nodes or scaling up to meet demand. 

04:15 UTC - Network engineering team initiating manual override to provision additional resolver instances and force capacity back to baseline.

04:30 UTC - New resolver instances reporting healthy. DNS query success rates rising. Downstream service connection errors beginning to clear.

04:36 UTC - All core metrics stabilized. DNS latency back to normal in Seoul region. Declaring mitigation. Handing off to infra team to investigate why the fleet size dropped and remained low.
Amazon.description: A configuration change in the Seoul region incorrectly removed the setting that specifies the minimum healthy hosts for the EC2 DNS resolver fleet, so the system fell back to a very low default. The fleet's healthy host count dropped and in-VPC DNS queries from EC2 instances failed for ~84 minutes until capacity was manually restored. AWS added semantic config validation and per-hour throttling on host removal as remediations.

generated.summary: A DNS resolution failure in the ap-northeast-2 region caused widespread service outages due to a failure in the auto-scaling mechanism for the internal VPC resolver fleet.
generated.root_cause: The VPC resolver fleet in ap-northeast-2 experienced an unexplained failure, and the auto-scaling group failed to replace unhealthy nodes or provision additional capacity, leading to complete DNS resolution timeouts.
timeline: 03:12 UTC: High rate of DNS resolution timeouts detected in ap-northeast-2.
03:18 UTC: Core application servers failed health checks due to inability to resolve internal VPC endpoints.
03:30 UTC: Resolver fleet metrics confirmed a significant decrease in active host count.
03:48 UTC: Confirmed auto-scaling was not replacing unhealthy nodes.
04:15 UTC: Network engineering initiated manual capacity provisioning.
04:30 UTC: New resolver instances stabilized and DNS success rates recovered.
04:36 UTC: Full service restoration and mitigation declared.
generated.impact: Users in the ap-northeast-2 region experienced severe service degradation and total loss of connectivity for internal dependencies, affecting multiple downstream database and API services.
action_items: Conduct a post-incident investigation to identify why the auto-scaling group failed to trigger node replacement.
Implement enhanced alerting for resolver fleet capacity thresholds and auto-scaling health check failures.
Develop a regional failover or fallback strategy for DNS resolution to mitigate dependency on a single VPC resolver pool.
Review and update the standard operating procedure for manual resolver fleet provisioning.

Amazon.reason: The AI incorrectly attributes the issue to an auto-scaling failure rather than the configuration change that removed the minimum healthy host setting, missing the critical root cause.
